metadata.school_name: University of Illinois Urbana Champaign Law School

analysis.name: Andrew Leipold
analysis.verdict: Great professor — tough but students love the teaching. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 23 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Professor Leipold is awesome. I was sad everytime the class ended. He gives a lot of hypos to understand the rules, and makes the expectations for the exam clear. I suspect that his exams got easier because I saw a lot of older complaints about how hard his exams were, but I did not feel this way and I don't think my classmates did neither.
Professor Leipold's lectures clear your concepts while making you laugh. I never bothered to open the textbook, his teachings were sufficient

Leipold is a such a gift to the world. You will learn so much, all while laughing and genuinely enjoying class. His examples are engaging and memorable. But most of all, he's incredibly caring and treats all of us with such respect: as if we each have something important to offer. Haven't met a single student who doesn't say they love Leipold.

I have not had a professor as good as Leipold, I loved every minute of class and learned so much. He is so funny that even if you're wrong, being cold-called in his class never catches you off guard, and so long as you do any reading, it is an absolute joy. Best professor hands down, the only downside is that he does not provide past exams.

Crim Pro: Investigation was my favorite class in law school so far (one semester left). The subject matter is fascinating because it touches on 4th, 5th, and 6th amendment issues. Leipold does an amazing job of relaying very complex information. Exam was tough-multiple-choice, true-false, and two issue-spotters. You really need to know your stuff!

analysis.name: Sean Anderson
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 9 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: The GOAT of UIUC law writing professors. The emperor of ERISA. The terror of Trusts and Estates. Once in a generation talent that you just don't see in this era. People have spent the GDP of small countries to have a chance to play trivia with him at the PILF auction, and word on the street is that it was worth every penny.

Professor Anderson is a solid professor. You DO NOT have read the books he assigns for the class--I would recommend borrowing them since he uses them sparingly. He cancels half the classes, which is nice. He is very helpful during office hours and answers pretty much any questions you have. He spends most time in class going over structural things.

Anderson is as condescending as they get. doesn't give adequate instructions. Any notes puts up on the screen regarding his strict preferences are taken down before you have a chance to note them. Make sure you go to office hours or else you will not get critical instruction that he gives everyone else in private & your grade will suffer for it.

The best writing professor at UIUC Law. Would take a full semester of this man's courses if possible. Cares about students and will gladly meet outside of class to discuss papers. He is also a 3x Jeopardy champion and a 2nd place finisher at the prestigious Blind Pig Trivia Night.

This semester Anderson switched his class to socratic method, but it doesn't take away from enjoying the class. He's a fantastic professor that makes learning about Trusts and Estates as interesting as one could. I would recommend taking any class of his if you get the chance.

analysis.name: Amitai Aviram
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 19 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Prof Aviram reads lengthy power points and talk endlessly instead of going straight to the point. His class is boring, less instructive and more of a show. His final is usually a take home exam and after he distributed arbitrary grades, he still not explain his rationale for grades nor is he persuasive about the legal problem and solution

Prof Aviram's BA class is different. You don't need to purchase a text book (yay) because he organized the material into (1) online lectures available on YouTube and (2) power point slides used in class, which include a wealth of practice questions. His lectures are very clear - an amazingly smart, kind, and helpful teacher. Will take again.

Professor Aviram is very nice in person outside of class. He seems to have all of his lectures memorized and can recite them to you in his office. He speaks very fast, teaches a difficult class, and teaches a large lecture 100+, but he manages to teach well nonetheless. I have never heard of extra credit for class participation though.

Just reads off of power point. Does not explain well. Does not seem to care if people show up or learn. Appears to be a good guy that assumes law students will do the work; he is wrong. The material could be interesting and he could convince people to chose this as a field to enter but he does nothing to encourage it.

If you want to save some money then this class is for you, no textbook required. Although you 'll feel miserable bc he does not explains that much during class, is expecting that we see some videos and then solve very abstract and complicated problems in class. No guidance whatsoever. the exam is a take home exam.

analysis.name: Rumana Alam
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 9 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: She was very demeaning and unclear.

It's alright overall, but grasping guidelines can be a bit challenging. On the bright side, attending her office hours is a lifesaver as it brings clarity. However, the downside is that everyone's flocking to her office hours, resulting in longer waits, especially before the final assignment deadlines. I don't think I would take their class again.
Ok class. Lots of work but not in a challenging way. She brings in cupcakes though. Textbooks are required but we barely touched them.

Professor Alam was a fantastic writing teacher. Her class was not easy, but I really appreciated how clear she was about what she wanted. Legal writing can be overwhelming to master without clear guidelines and steps and she took a ton of stress off me by providing these steps. You will know what to expect, so if you work hard you will love her.

Alam is the best writing prof here. She's not easy, but she is very fair and makes her expectations abundantly clear. She also actually cares about her students' development. Her slides are very helpful, and she sings to her class and brings snacks.

analysis.name: Aylon Schulte
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 6 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Knows what makes a good brief and memo but goes out of her way to ensure she's not holding your hand in the course to the point of making things unnecessarily difficult. It's one thing to try and teach your students to be independent, it's another to consistently hide the ball.

Possibly the best professor I've ever had. She is firm, demanding, but fair, and while she expects you to figure out legal writing somewhat on your own, she will support you entirely along the way. Nothing but respect for Professor Schulte, she easily outclasses most other professors here in terms of professionalism, pedagogy, and accessibility.

It is true that Professor Schulte makes things unnecessarily difficult. She gives vague guidance on your papers, and will not point out simple things like certain criteria being missed on your paper. Instead, she expects you to figure it out on your own. You could get the same experience by just reading the text and submitting memos.

I felt like I did not learn as much as I should have in this class.We wasted time playing games in class a lot.Also,she wouldn't just teach you how to do stuff, you were supposed to figure it all out on your own,and then lose points when you didn't do it right. Grading seemed random too. She is personally a nice enough person though.

analysis.name: Kenworthey Bilz
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews — check if this fits your learning style. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: This was literally THE worst class I've taken in law school. The professor simply talks at you and doesn't really include the readings for class. Moreover, she regularly misunderstands students' questions. But the biggest issue is how disrespectful she is.

Professor Bilz turns criminal law into a psychology/sociology class. She talks ad nauseum about deterrence rationales. While she talks very little about black letter law. Unfortunately, her exam is substantially black letter law. A good supplement is a requirement if you expect to do well in her class. Because she is not a very good teacher.
A lot of stuff in the reading packet and a lot of digressions in the lectures obscured the law that we were really supposed to be learning for the exam and made the experience unenjoyable
She is obviously brilliant, but she was not a great teacher. But, she is friendly and approachable. Just don't expect to learn much from her in class.

I really liked Professor Bilz, as well as her lectures which contained a lot of helpful hypos and explanations. As long as you follow closely what she is teaching, those hypos are not that hard to answer. I also liked her reasoning in explaining difficult concepts. Definitely enjoyed her class.

analysis.name: Francis Boyle
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews — check if this fits your learning style
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 22 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Was extremely interested in International Law, but did not learn anything by attending class - had to teach myself everything by reading the book & commercial supplements. We had a paper in lieu of a final exam, which was nice, but every class was like a verbal flogging. He's very passionate and knowledgeable about the subject but too acerbic.

I learned nothing in a semester of Jurisprudence, except a lot of tin-foil hat ranting about the Pope and Newt Gingrich. He will use a mic in a small lecture hall, then scream into it the entire class. Expect migraines, and worse-than-1L belittling over questions with no answers. Don't expect actual legal philosophy or history.
Lectures most of the class and doesn't believe in Socratic Method since it breeds moral relativism.

Francis A. Boyle (or as he calls himself: "FAB") is a left-wing radical who spent 50% of his classtime name-dropping about what "famous" professors from Harvard and Chicago that he studied under.  DON'T TAKE HIM --- IT'S A WASTE OF TIME.
Class was a waste of time.  Spent the whole time babbling.

analysis.name: Ralph Brubaker
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 12 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Great Professor. He makes the subject fun and clear, but he's demanding. If he calls on you and you're not prepared he will not let go for most of the class. He always gives you everything you need to answer in class as long as you did your work. Can be intimidating for some, but has a great sense of humor. The final will be tough.

Ks without Bru would've been absolutely miserable, but he made the class fun.  Read the material though, because he calls on you and does not relent if you're unprepared.  Great guy, and easy to talk to outside of class.

Contracts with The Bru = many future contract scholars. High interest class. Socratic method: he's not out to get you, but _read_ the material. He knows. He'll hit 3-4 students per class. Final is Tough. Recommend BLL outline supplement, but not absolutely necessary. He's clear about what he wants you to learn and what he wants on his final.

Bru is a legendary K's professor at UofI and one of the best in the country. He can be a bit intimidating and his lectures can be extremely dry, however he is really goofy on occasion which makes the class palatable. Overall he's a great professor and I'd be happy to take another course with him.

Reads from an outline from 2006. Boring and unenthusiastic. Keeps you 15 minutes over time every class. Doesn't take questions during class or does a review session. Worst teacher in higher education I have ever had.

analysis.name: Steven Beckett
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 11 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Professor Beckett and I never got along on a personal level. We had more than our fair share of disagreements. He was arrogant and authoritarian.  However, it wasn't until I got out into the real world that I realized he taught me more than any three other classes I took in law school combined.

Lives to hear himself talk about his glory days that he calls "war stories" at cost to quality of our education.  Full of himself, and yet not even that intellectual of a professor.  Demands daily attendance, asks irrelevant questions that he thinks are profound/have depth, doesn't teach from the book.  Not recommended, an utter waste of tuition.

He is a brilliant trial attorney but not a brilliant professor. He is well-respected and deserves it. However, he is an attorney turned professor that has not mastered the intricacies of explaining material, lecturing, or writing exams. The #1 student in our class received a B; several students in the bottom half received A's. Arbitrary grading

His disorganization is exceeded only by his arrogance.  He and Gunsales made this class a waste due to their complete lack of organization and inflexibility.  If you value your time, skip this class, because unless they completly overhaul the entire course (not likely given the egos involved) you won't do anything productive all semester.

Arrogance, disorganization, waste of time, and poor work, hurt what could have been an otherwise good course. If you like knowing what you're graded on, don't take this class. If you value your time, don't take this class. If you are smart, you won't take this class.

analysis.name: Bruce Smith
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 22 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: People that didn't like Bruce Smith are the same people that prefer LeBron over Jordan. They are Un-American and not to be trusted.
Excellent professor. Probably the best lecturer at U of I. Extremely intelligent and articulate. Has high expectations and can be intimidating but he means well.

Great teacher. Intimidating and scary at first, but you get used to his fast talking speed and sarcastic remarks. Always learn something and feel intelligently and mentally challenged and drained after each class. He's most clear about his exam and grading process. Nice person in office hour. DON'T ask questions during class. Only he gets to do it

Prof. Smith was the highlight of law school for me. He is brilliant, and I never left class without feeling challenged and having learned something. He is a very fair and actually very kind and funny person. Only those students who didn't respect him or others, or who didn't prepare were chastised by him (and rightfully so).

Fantastic teacher. Probably the best actual teacher I had in law school.  Only rude if you deserved it.  Took different positions just for the sake of argument/education.  Crystal clear in what he wanted you to know.

analysis.name: Rene Bowser
analysis.verdict: Solid pick — well-liked with reasonable difficulty. But trending upward recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 3 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: He was really funny, but not in a way that most people understood. Confusing because classes are so easy enough for middle schoolers to understand, but test were graded extremely hard. Can't believe I ended up with a B

Great professor.  Just relax and listen to what he has to say and you'll be fine - people get too worked up over minor discrepancies in the book.  Nicest guy at the school.  You are in good hands for Torts and Crim.
funny and doesnt take himself too seriously.  no interest in humiliation

analysis.name: George Bell
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Sweet guy. The class itself is pretty boring, but very easy. I really like Professor Bell. He can be really funny sometimes. He might not remember your name, even by midterms week, but he's nice! The only downside - papers due on saturday nights.
Prof. Bell is probably the best professor I've had in college.  He is extremely nice and really cares about people. I would recommend anyone to take one of his classes.
Overall just a really nice guy who has a big interest in what he actually teaches about.

analysis.name: Lynn Branham
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 3 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: WORST PROFESSOR EVER!  Her laziness is shocking for a law professor.
fantastic.  u of i needs her back.

analysis.name: Christine Hurt
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 10 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Business Associations at SMU. Extremely unorganized. Unprepared & wants students to teach. Slides are irrelevant/unhelpful. Unclear expectations. 4 cred class felt like 8 cred class via difficulty & amount of work. Exam questions are unrelated to lectures. GPA killer unless you put your other grades on the line for her class. Prepare to self teach

She taught Kaplan Bar Review.  The worst 6 hours of my life.  She speaks to the camera like she is speaking to children.  And skips important and relevant topics with cheeky remarks.  Further, she says the word okay 3 times a minute, at least...Sometimes she will end a sentence with "ok" and begin the next with "ok."  TERRIBLE!!

biggest waste of time so far in law school.  She dumbs things down in class and then expects you to understand and apply complicated concepts that she never taught on the exam.  The casebook was also absolutely terrible.
Super nice and funny. Sometimes moves a bit slowly, and as a result gets behind on the syllabus. Final was mostly MCQ's and a few short essays. The material sucks, but she makes it bearable.
She is more interested in entertainment than teaching. She will never give you a clear answer on a question. She likes to hide the ball. She likes to make you feel like crap.

analysis.name: Jamelle C. Sharpe
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge. But trending upward recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Really, really hard class & topic generally. His exams are not easy either, but he is very fair, organized, & runs a great class. Great if you want to learn a tough but important area. Very legit.

Humorous, professional, and responsible. I took 2 courses from him, administrative law and federal court. His courses cover a lot of legislative history which is what I liked because it told me where did those rules come from. But you need to be well prepared before classes otherwise you could get lost very easily. The best professor I ever met.

Fantastic Professor, very conscientious and clear in explaining every last detail. Very difficult, however - class requires lots of memorization on top of difficult concepts. Nice guy who is always available. Also humorous and entertaining.

Liked him. Kind man. I remember all those case names because of his slides. He really likes to see students during office hours. We had lunch with him and got to talk to him for two or three hours. Answers all questions.
The best professor at UIUC Law. Thought admin law would be boring, but pretty much my favorite class I’ve taken here. Great sense of humor.

analysis.name: John Colombo
analysis.verdict: Great professor — tough but students love the teaching. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 34 reviews — fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: There's no doubt Colombo is a great professor, undoubtedly one of the best at the law school.  He's extremely clear and easy to understand.  The downside, however, is that he's full of himself.  Not enough space to go into details, but several incidents make that apparent.  I guess most people here, being law students, just don't notice or care.

Colombo is great. Material may not be fun but he explains it thoroughly. Took him for income tax and tax exempt organizations. In both classes he was very clear on the material, and exams were both VERY fair without any surprises. Likes to repeat himself a lot in class but it helps emphasize what you need to know. Definitely take his classes!

Colombo is the best. He is a great professor and an overall nice guy...his class is not easy by any stretch of the imagination though, he really challenges you in class to make the arguments both ways and if you don't think about the material and reading prior to class you will be lost. His exam is extremely fair no surprises.

I was not looking forward to this class, but Colombo made it one of my favorites.  Takes difficult concepts and explains them in a way easy to understand even for non-finance/accounting types like myself.  The material is tough, but he does a phenomenal job teaching it and I learned a lot in this class.

Professor Colombo is by far the best professor I have ever had in law school. He teaches you every single thing you need to know, makes it interesting and relevant, and makes very difficult topics extremely clear and understandable. Fair exam and fair grading. It does not get better than this folks...

analysis.name: Charles Tabb
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 11 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Very useful material. Funny guy. A bit too smarmy and pleased with himself sometimes. However, it's pretty funny most of the time. Has a great moustache. The entire course is multiple choice, which is pretty rad for me.

Having a professor this dedicated to his students and passionate about teaching is rare at this level. Not only is he an absolute genius in his field but he goes above and beyond to help students. His exam was an intense 4 hour marathon but it was fair and he graded every exam in about a week and gave grades out early, which is a HUGE relief.

I thought Tabb was good.Class was organized very well, and Tabb is very clear when explaining things.Uses fun examples and hypos.Encourages class participation and never treats you like your completely retarded.Test was VERY straight forward. Look for a remedies question defintely.

Tabb is a boring teacher;not dynamic or engaging;He gets the concepts across just fine;seems concerned with students really learning;invites Q's;test aimed at major concepts in class-definitely manageable;out of class interaction with students-marginal

Professor Tabb is a wonderful Professor whom everybody loves. He is extremely knowledgeable, respectful and genuine. Attending his bankruptcy class is an unmatched learning experience. Classes are very clear and organized. Favorite Professor of all.

analysis.name: Cynthia Williams
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: I agree with the insecurity part. However, Professor Williams is very knowledgable and clear. She helped me learn torts for sure and it helps me to this day so no complaints there. However, she does seem insecure and whines a lot. I think some normal activities in her life may help...about as exciting as Nixon at the beach. Just read the material!

Professor Williams knows her stuff and is very articulate.  Typing what she says is like reading from a well-organized book.  However, she is also very emotional and insecure, which is reflected in the  condescending way in which she sometimes addresses the students.  This made often made class unbearable.  She needs to get some perspective!

This class is relatively easy. The major concepts/rules you need to learn are found in the cases. In class she reinforces some of the main concepts you need to know. If you create a good outline you should do fine. The MC on the exam is a little difficult but the essay questions are very straight forward and fair.

Does not teach, class is just for about tangential discussions.  She has literally said that explaining the materials is "not her job" in class.  There have even been moments of open dissent, where students have raised their hands and asked her to stop wasting everyone's time.  Do NOT take BA1 with her.

analysis.name: David Hyman
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. But trending upward recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: I took Professor Hyman in Georgetown Law.  His class differs greatly from other CivPro classes.  Most other professors focus on the technical aspects of the rules (it IS a procedure class, after all), Hyman focuses on the why.  Why was this rule made? Why did the lawyer bring the case in this court? Once you get that part straight his class is easy

Professor Hyman is a gentleman and a wonderful professor. His lectures are extremely clear and organized, and he is very respectful in answering students' questions. His office hours are also extremely helpful, and I often find myself communicating with him rather than simply being told the answer. He is definitely a first-class Professor.

Sometimes it seemed like he really enjoyed making students feel stupid in class.  On the other hand, he's obviously brilliant.  Teaching was always clear and organized, even if it wasn't always interesting.

Civil Procedure at Georgetown Law. Some people found him funny, but I thought he was offensive. He made jokes about people becoming alcoholics because of their mothers. I didn't think it was funny. He didn't seem to care about his students. His exam was almost all policy. I have to admit that he knows the material well.

analysis.name: Daniel Hamilton
analysis.verdict: Solid pick — well-liked with reasonable difficulty
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: AMAZING TEACHER!!! Seriously, one of the best teachers I have ever had.  He is INCREDIBLY enthusiastic, hilarious, and discusses things so thoroughly that any confusion you could possibly  Con Law will be completely cleared up. He will tell you what is on the test and TEACH YOU EVERYTHING you could possibly need. Everyone in my section loved him!!!

Hamilton is the man.  The class is broken down so that every aspect fits into neatly made categories that he sets out in the beginning of the year.  He really makes sure that everyone understands the material and is familiar with what will be on the exam.  Best professor I had this year.
I had him for property and legal history, and he's fantastic!  If you have any choice in the matter, don't graduate without taking his classes.  You'll be happy you did.
amazing professor with full of patience, clear explaining and helpful way

analysis.name: Ellen Kordik
analysis.verdict: Solid pick — well-liked with reasonable difficulty. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 19 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: I like professor Kordik as a person. However, I did not really gain much from her classes other than what I had to teach myself. The room will be easier since she doesn't do much of cold calling, but you will be teaching yourself everything in the class.

I am disturbed that a professor who writes a final exam with TONS of errors, year after year, can sneak by. Not even to mention how little we learned this semester, and how much of the material she ran out of time to cover. The ABA would be HORRIFIED if they audited this course.

I liked Kordik. The final exam was hard--she covers a lot of material in the class that does not appear on MPRE practice exams, be warned! She has six "reflection papers" which are two double-spaced pages where you give your opinion on a certain situation that implicates legal ethics. She doesn't cold-call, and readings mostly aren't necessary.

Kordik is the worst professor I've ever taken. Her lectures are unbearably boring. She has a weak grasp of the materials. She regularly misunderstands students' questions. All her written materials are full of typos and substantive errors, even her final exams. The only upside is her final is multiple choice, which I do better at than essays.

Class moved slowly and was a bit tedious (though, that could have been the subject matter).  Prof. Kordik was very clear and did nice recaps.  There was a lot of class discussion and she called on everyone, even a gunner who repeatedly had only negative things to say about the ADA and were just trying to get a class participation grade boost.

analysis.name: Eric Freyfogle
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews — check if this fits your learning style. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 12 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Freyfogle is a wealth of knowledge. If all you're interested in is black letter law, take a different class. But, if you're interested in policy discussions and hilarious and highly informative tangents, then you will love his class. Students should check out his articles and books - he's a great writer.

Freyfogle's classes are interesting and informative, as long as you do the reading then you will learn something. If you're not interested in wider issues then don't bother turning up - you're probably boring. He is prone to tangential discussions, but these are commonly very interesting. All-in-all a great professor who has plenty of time for you.

I've done 11 reviews and this is my first negative one.  In all honesty he seems like he may be senile.  He just talks about what he wants, and there is very little, if any, teaching involved in his classes.  He's nice enough, but in a crazy Uncle sort of way.  I got an A, but if I hadn't I probably would be writing a much angrier review

I'm sitting in this class right now.  Class ends in 14 minutes and we still have not discussed the assigned reading.  Last week we ended class early to attend a talk, only he rambled on so long that we had to show up to the talk late.  The guy is full of himself and never shuts up.  Seems easy enough though.

i am not sure if 'incomprehensible" describes what this man brings to the table in class each day.  what a waste of time.  go talk to some random homeless person in the park, you'll learn as much about the law from him or her.

analysis.name: Margareth Etienne
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time — consider alternatives if possible. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 24 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Subject of discussions were very loose and disorganized.  As a person who is now in criminal law full time, it is amazing how little she taught me.  Better to use just black letter outlines.

You are on your own! Get supplemental books and learn everything yourself because following her lectures/explanations will land you in a mess of conflicting information. She's very nice but seems too busy to look anything up and instead gives information off the top of her head that is often wrong. Doesn't show up to office hours or reply to email.

Terrible. Worst professor I've had in law school. Etienne seems completely disinterested in the subject she's teaching. Lectures are hard to follow and she wastes a lot time calling on people who are not prepared. She is not good at answering questions. Worst of all, she was terrible at explaining the differences between common law and the MPC.

Outside of class, she's really nice, but she's just not a good prof. There was too much opportunity for students to share irrelevant opinions and thoughts. She was rarely able to answer questions effectively, mostly because she didn't appear to know her subject very well. I taught myself the class from the text/supplement.  The final was fair.

I thought she was great!  Sure, she doesn't use much socratic method (only to begin class), but it's nice to have a break from the fear of other classes.  And unlike some other profs, Etienne Was Actually a Real Trial Lawyer for a number of years.  She's smart, very friendly, and fills her classes with fun tidbits about actual Trial Practice.

analysis.name: Eric Johnson
analysis.verdict: Great professor — tough but students love the teaching. But trending upward recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 10 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Johnson was an outstanding Evidence professor. He gives you a workbook filled with problems that he will go over in class, so that helps you prep for the final. He is highly accessible, and since we finished the class early, he made the last classes open office hours. Evidence is tough, but he does a pretty good job of explaining the concepts.

Evidence Expert. The exam was extremely similar to the in class hypospractice workbook we do everyday, so keep up with those and there will be no surprises. Closed note, so much confrontation clause. Likes to cold call.

Johnson was one of my favorite professors 1L year. The material is confusing, because of the dichotomy between common law and Model Penal Code, but he does his best to explain the concepts clearly. Exam was a little tough-three questions that were sort of like issue-spotters and a reasonable multiple choice section. Very helpful during office hours

Johnson is a great professor and very easy to talk to.  He was clear in discussing the material.  Like the poster below said, it can be confusing because you bounce between the model penal code and common law, but he does a good job.  He's not there to trick you--he really wants you to learn and understand the material.  A++

Professor Johnson is the best!  He brings a ton of real world experience to the classroom.  He was so nice to everyone in class and was super available.  If I was more interested in anything related to criminal law, I would definitely take more classes with him.  The exam was rough, but really fair.

analysis.name: Emily Grant
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 7 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Enjoyable Decedents summer class.  Fun, interesting, and final wasn't bad.
Painfully tedious & boring subject, but Prof. Grant was great.

analysis.name: Matthew Finkin
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 8 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Prof. Finkin is the most underrated professor in the entire law school.  People are scared of his classes because they are challenging.  Guess what, law school is challenging.  So quit taking classes you can sleep through and take a test at the end of the term and take a class where you just might learn something.

Everyone should take him. He's one of the most accomplished professors here, but people avoid him because labor is a dying field and because he is tough. He demands a lot, but I'd rather have a professor who pushes than one who slacks off. I have no interest in practicing labor, but his lectures kept everyone engaged/interested.. and terrified.

I took labor and employee rights with him. Can be very abrasive, but it's never personal, and if you can adapt to it then he's one of the best profs at the school. He's upfront about both the black letter law and the reality in practice. If you're interested in labor/emp, he is more than ready to help you. Just don't skip a reading.

Easy & interesting intersession class. If you don't like labor law, this is the best labor law class for you. He will go off topic a lot, embrace it. He will also look for people to respond rather than cold call. It will get awkward if no one answers. Just answer. The questions are not hard.

After reading some of these posts I think a lot of them are very fair. That said, many are from 2018 and older. I think his tough attitude has turned into more of an abrasive and sometimes rude professor. He is extremely smart but will not be taking him again.

analysis.name: Jeff Ginger
analysis.verdict: Most students had a tough time — consider alternatives if possible. But trending upward recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 15 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Course is disorganized and grading policy is unclear. The assignments are long and tedious so be prepared to be working on homework for around 3-7 hours a week (maybe more). Participation is super strict in this class, so be prepared to talk often. There's a group project the end. Please do it with someone you know. Will save you:)Enthusiastic guy!

Unfortunately, this class burdens students with weekly unclear, dense project assignments. Attendance strict, late work penalized heavily. The workload is excessive, projects overly difficult, lacking practicality, hindering learning. Jeff seems quite nice, but class is unstructured with complicated/confusing instructions and is unaccommodating.

Ginger makes class unnecessarily difficult. He claims he has taught this class many times but calls us "guinea pigs" every week. He doesn't even know how to set up the Canvas or the Zoom. Although lecture is not necessary, difficulty comes in the contradictory hw instructions and stubborn policies. Does not seem knowledgable about what he teaches.

Good guy, he cares, he's passionate. People giving him a 1 over wordy descriptions in the HW is not fair. Put forth effort and Professor Ginger will do what he can to support.

I wish email communication was more open, but in general, good caring professor.

This class was more of an overview than expected. It's mostly homework projects and no big tests.  There's a lot of flexibility and choice on assignments but they're long. I liked the making the user flow, figma prototype and scratch game. It's a good class if you're new to coding and like doing projects with art or if you want to try AI.

analysis.name: Heidi Hurd
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 44 reviews — fairly reliable

review_highlights.comment: She is probably a better professor than she was dean (because her decision of dismissing me from the law school in 2006 likely cost the State of Illinois billions of dollars (likely opportunity cost of my productive capacity as opposed to being treated as primarily a mental health patient in some University of Illinois experiment). Enjoy Australia.

High interest class - Hurd is a compelling presenter. Her Socratic method is not intended to embarrass and she hits several students per class rather than 1 or 2. She will do her best to make your answer forward the discussion. Supplements are less helpful, but still necessary (E&E). Heavy on judges/theory. Final was very fair, fun and difficult.

Professor Hurd is a wonderful Professor and she is an extremely kind-hearted person who always tries her best to not make students feel stupid. She likes encouraging students and her lectures are extremely organized. She is also very dedicated to our learning and even held a whole-day Q&A session before exam this semester. Absolutely love her!

Prof. Hurd is extremely charismatic, brilliant, and fun.  Students who are less comfortable with philosophy find the class difficult, but I enjoyed it immensely.  She really makes you think about the tough questions, about why the law should be or is one way or another. Outside of class, I found her approachable and willing to talk.  Loved it!

She can be hard to follow in class, but the idea that she teaches "philosophy" instead of the law in 1L classes shows only that you miss the point of the class & the law, in which the reasoning behind a rule is just as, if not more, important than the rule itself. Also, she gave me an A for a paper obviously disagreeing with her viewpoint???

analysis.name: Jennifer Robbennolt
analysis.verdict: Solid pick — well-liked with reasonable difficulty
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 14 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Professor Robbennolt is the bomb. If you get her as your Torts prof., you have been blessed. Like, I hate math and don't care about empirical research, but she made me consider taking Empirical Methods in Law because she's the prof. for that class. She is that awesome. Just know that the exam will kick your rear end no matter how much you prepare.

Awesome Prof. extremely clear about the material and what you need to know for the exam. Do not underestimate her exam though, it was the hardest one I had my first semester by far. The MC questions are extremely difficult, make sure you know the jurisdictional splits or you will be screwed. Very nice person inside and outside of class!

Genuinely friendly and affable with a great sense of humor. Her voice is rather grating but it's helpful because she's more voluminous when about to say something of import. She's very clear about what you need to know and doesn't humiliate you when you're called on. Very approachable outside of class. Highly recommended.
Great lecturer and made the class fun. Would highly recommend.

Professor Robbennolt is wonderful. She cares a LOT about students' success. You will be very lucky to get placed in her section! Don't be deceived by how enjoyable Torts is, though... the final is by far one of the hardest ones I've had in law school.

analysis.name: Jason Mazzone
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 15 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Jason Mazzone is a very difficult professor to recommend. He requires students to purchase multiple supplemental materials in addition to the textbook. In class, he frequently uses cold calls in a way that feels shaming rather than pedagogical, and his manner be rude and dismissive. His exams seem designed more to showcase his own cleverness.

Really cool guy. He might come across as intimidating, but he genuinely loves this stuff and just wants to engage with you. Wish I realized that sooner! Only quirk is that 80% ish of his cold calls are to men only. Several guys even got cold called multiple times throughout the semester, while most women never got cold called the entire semester.

I am studying for the bar and Mazzone prepared us the least out of all our professors. Without Barbri, I would be lost on Con Law. Mazzone seems more interested in torturing his students with cold calls, excessive reading assignments, and a final exam three times longer than the regular law school exam, rather than actually teaching.

I do not think that the participation grade adjustment, which is very significant at up to +-2/3 of a letter grade, was administered in anything resembling an objective manner. Instead, I suspect those few who happened to gain his favor on certain days got a large increase in their grade.

Mazzone has a very unique class compared to other 1L courses. You are not allowed to use a computer. However, this is great for class discussion and participation. He loves that. He can come off as condescending, but is really a sweet man. He grades very fairly. Even though he warns against supplements, many As & Bs use Chemerinsky's successfully.

analysis.name: Jennifer Pahre
analysis.verdict: Solid pick — well-liked with reasonable difficulty. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 21 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Phenomenal professor, she truly cares about her students and tailors her lectures to keeping class interesting. Very easy class with limited quizzes, midterms that the TA's give a lot of direction on if you ask, and extra credit to help you out. Makes law relevant for even those not wanting a career in it. TAKE THIS CLASS you will not regret it.

She is my favorite professor by far. Not only does she care about her students but she tries to make the material as enjoyable as possible. She even brought a class of 200 apple cider donuts FRESHLY baked from curtis apple orchard. She goes out of her way to make her students feel special and she truly does a great job of teaching.

She is an excellent teacher. No matter your view on the American legal system, it will interest you. She uses a lot of real life examples, often examples that are funny or bizarre. There were two papers (mid-term and final) and five online quizzes. Read the book and you should be fine. ATTENDANCE IS REQUIRED. NO FINAL EXAM.

One of the best professors I've had. You did not need to attend lectures, but they were really good so many students attended. She is a true expert in the field and really inspires students to pursue the law. There were 2 extra credit papers.

Lectures are very interesting, but can run fast at times. She makes good use of videos to support the lectures. She is very knowledgeable about the law, and is always willing to answer student questions. Four midterms are online (open-note) and final is a relatively easy, five-page paper. Papers are graded hard, so make sure to make time for those.

analysis.name: Janice Pea
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 17 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Prof. Pea is a good professor, but I felt like she was very interested in students who spent a lot of effort sucking up to her, and had no interest in students who didn't suck up but still had questions. Also, I think it's bad form that she gives out a take home & in-class exam for her classes-it's like having an extra class during exam period.

Professor Pea is one of the best writing professors you could ask for at Illinois. You know she is offering you good advice since this is what she does for a living, essentially writing the law. She expects a lot from her students, but this is all for your benefit. Make the most out of your time with her, it will be well worth it.

Just awful in every single way. Doesn't know the material and has no interest in teaching out side of bragging about herself. Also likes to talk about drug use in class, as if we're supposed to believe she smokes pot and that somehow if she did that would make her cool.

No teaching going on as far as what we are actually supposed to do. It was only after the fact through trial and error that we learned about the expectations for our papers. This teacher tends to favor the students that suck up to her.
This class is the most unpleasant part of law school. The material is fine, but compared to other teachers for this class we have twice the amount of work, with half the instruction.

analysis.name: Jacqueline Ross
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews — check if this fits your learning style. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 16 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Ross certainly knows her stuff, only wish she had any idea of how to convey it. She reads from a prepared script, moving a mile-a-minute quoting rules of evidence by their numbers. No joke, she actually has a written script. Cold calls, but doesn't know anyone. Began skipping most classes & studied a review book instead- did well on final anyway.

Not the quality of teacher that U of I requires.  She does forget names, she starts hypo's and frequently tells students they are wrong because of facts she forgot to mention, and often loses track of where we are.  She also seems to choose 3 people to constantly call on and unlike other Professors this is not due to the quality of their answers.

Professor Ross is clearly smart, but a very poor teacher.  She did not explain concepts well, did not know the names of the students in her class, refused to discuss the exam until very late in the semester, wrote a dreadful exam which tested very little of what she taught, and refused to allow access to the Rules of Evidence during the exam.
she seemed pretty scatterbrained.  very nice and intelligent, but not the best professor

Just random grading. A lot of us that got As in every other class got Bs and B+s. The final was terribly written. When asked to go over the exam, she just sent over a mock answer instead of meeting and explaining why she gave the grade she got.

analysis.name: Richard Kaplan
analysis.verdict: Solid pick — well-liked with reasonable difficulty. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 19 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: He has no knowledge of real-life transactions, when you ask him about it, he gets angry and asks you to talk to the TA, who has no work experience and knows less than him. The sessions with the TA were bad, he just read from a note and had no in-depth knowledge of the subject.

Elder Law is a fun class, but be warned, the exam is tough. Kaplan lectures nearly the entire class, so it's easy to fall asleep, but on the final, he will toss in facts from the lecture that are NOT in the two textbooks that accompany the class. You don't really need to do the reading-it's super complex and uber-detailed. Exam is 50 MC/90 minutes.

Prof. Kaplan is a brilliant scholar who translates difficult concepts for students. If you need help or do not understand an issue, he takes time to review with you, and when you are done talking to him, you have the concept down. Next to research and writing, his elder law class is the most practical and useful course I had in law school.

Kaplan is great and tries his best to make income tax an understandable subject. As the semester goes on he sometimes assumes we know more than we do and it can lead to some confusion going over problems. He's very accessible for office hours though and friendly even outside of class where he'll greet students in the hall if he sees them

Prof. Kaplan is dedicated to students and he takes time to break down difficult concepts. Tax is a difficult subject, but his teaching style is so engaging that one becomes intrigued by the tax implications of almost everything. One of the best professors at the law school, but definitely the best in my opinion.

analysis.name: Robin Kar
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 20 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Kar is an amazing professor who is funny and extremely caring. He always asks students for feedback to ensure that he isn't moving too fast, and makes sure to read the faces of his students to see if he is making sense. Do the reading, show up to class, be prepared to be called on (lots of cold calling), and you will do just fine.

Professor Kar is a very caring professor who takes time outside of the classroom to have review sessions, which is very helpful. However, his final is closed-book, which is pretty ridiculous and unfair considering the sheer amount of material the course covers. I really think his final should not be closed book.

He has done some excellent work, empowerment theory is genuinely so good, but you will spend 45 mins of a 75 min class talking about things which are not the law. When he goes into his eighth tangent and you zone out you will miss the next rule, then he will go on another tangent and you will miss the next rule. This repeats until the end of time.

He cares about students and gives a good basis of contract law. He wants you to be ready for his exams and helped students feel well prepared. Although I wish he spent a little less time on facts of cases unrelated to contracts, I think he brought it around and left us with an understanding of what the rules were.

He can be really funny at times. But I learned little in his class. He goes off on tangents and gets distracted by students' never-ending hypo questions, so the learning in each 75-minute class is probably 15 minutes tops. Fyi...he constantly clears his throat IN the microphone at volume 10000000000/10. Ear plugs, stay in your lane self-teaching

analysis.name: Patrick Keenan
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews — check if this fits your learning style. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 12 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: I had Professor Keenan in the first semester of 2008.  tyhe class is pretty laid back and not very challenging.  however, be prepared to essentially just hear the textbook repeated back to you in the form of powerpoint slides.  that aspect of the class is frustrating because it makes the class very hard to attend.

Amazing professor - lectures are really interesting and he seems to genuinely care about students opinions/understanding of the material. Hes really passionate about the classes he teaches and that definitely comes through during lecture. Grading is fair. One of the best professors at the law school
A friend highly recommended Professor Keenan.  I am not at all happy with that friend right now.

I really enjoyed Prof. Keenan's class. He worked with those he called on and was respectful of those who commented in class. He brought personal insights to various issues and would use currents events to supplement the casebook. Also is well-liked outside of the classroom by many students.

He didn't answer questions fully, ran circles, didn't seem to understand what students were saying and was generally boring.  Perhaps it was just a clash in teaching style with other profs here (he was visiting from U of I), but his style did not sit well with most students.

analysis.name: Jay Kesan
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Kesan is a great person and very jovial, but his lectures for Civ Pro were very unclear. The amount of confusion in the class before the final was ridiculous. He also comes off as very arrogant and often dismisses the answers of students if he thinks your questions are stupid.
good in-class professor, but is rarely available outside of class.
Really nice guy. great prof. one of my favorites at law school.

analysis.name: Kurt Lash
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge. But trending upward recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 5 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Very clear, outlines the material for you and is very engaging in class. He warns you  if he's going to cold call you.   My only complaints are he assigns unrealistically heavy reading 1 day before class and then kind of glosses over it in class. Also he didn't mention anything about the exam until 2 days before. Surprise! Its closed book!

Amazing! Clear in his expectations for understanding the material and makes con law interesting. Spends a lot of time outlining the class. The readings were sufficient, but definitely not excessive. Only drawback is he tends to go slow and not always clear on what readings we will get to in class. A truly smart, interesting man.

Lash was my favorite professor 1L year. He basically creates your outline for you. He repeats a lot of the same material, which is helpful. Also super helpful in office hours and he genuinely cares about his students. His exam was very fair and he covered the topics he emphasized during the semester-due process, state action, suit against executive
Shoved his political opinions down the throats of everyone in class.
Best professor at the law school. The final was very fair.

analysis.name: Lesley Wexler
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 9 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Makes a fairly straightforward topic confusing- only class I needed a supplement. She does try with her pop culture references, but even that became predictable and boring. If you volunteer she will stick with you for the class, which really hurts classroom discussion. She means well, but no contest - the worst class and professor I had this year.

Professor Wexler was an ok professor. She runs through a lot of hypotheticals in the class. The exam was open-book, but it was very tough. She releases most of her recent exams, but not the exam answers. Exam is very rigorous-two issue-spotters with different types of torts-first was more general and second was focused on product liability.

Professor Wexler is a really good teacher who takes the time to make sure you understand the material. She gives a review of the previous class at the beginning of class which was really helpful for outlining. The exam was also very fair and she really prepares you for the content. Be sure to read the note sections at the end of the cases!
She's tough, but super nice and funny. She'll take the time to answer your questions and meet with you. You better read though... AND DON'T BE LATE

I am so happy I had Dean Wexler for torts. Her lectures are interesting, she uses current events, and does an excellent job of utilizing pictures with her lectures (this is significant for visual learners and memory). She encouraged us to think for ourselves and provided guidance when it was clear no one had any idea what was going on. 10/10

analysis.name: Robert Lawless
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Lawless is the goat. Bankruptcy is extremely difficult, but I find myself enjoying the class. Hes made the bankruptcy field accessible by recording short summaries of each chapter and being really accessible (through both our discussion board and office hours). Hes clearly passionate about the topic, and that helps in paying attention in class.

Professor Lawless is a smart man, but can't teach very well at all.  He assigns tons of cases that he never talks about, then he expects almost expert-level knowledge of these very poorly written code section while delving into difficult problems that he explains way too quickly.  Two classes like this would be more work than a full-time job.

Professor Lawless is very friendly and he is always willing to help students with questions either after class or during office hours. His lectures contain a lot of practical materials that help us understand how the topic actually works in practice. Half of the lecture will consist of doing problem sets. Definitely loved the class!

Prof. Lawless does expect a lot form his students-to do the reading, attend the lectures and participate in class.  But he gives more than  that in return and makes a real effort to know all of his students.  No one is more helpful on this faculty.

analysis.name: Laurie Reynolds
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 12 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: I liked Reynolds a lot. She is super-energetic, and passionate. However, I think she spends way too much time talking about policy, even though her exam is pure black-letter law. I had a terrific TA when I took Property and she laid out the analysis for the different situations, or else I would not have passed her class. She provides many old exams
This woman is awesome! She's bubbly, energetic, fun and at her age she still gets excited about the law and comments students make.

Most people adore Professor Reynolds, she's extremely kind, passionate about what she does, and is always willing to help students. Unfortunately her teaching style just didn't work for me. It was tough to take good notes, and sometimes she speaks so fast you can't process what she's saying. Exam is closed book.

Fantastic Prof!  She doesn't spoon feed you black letter(HER casebook does that).  In class it's all discussion, but she guides it carefully in the right direction.  Take one of her classes before graduating (State & Local, or Land Use Planning), you'll be glad you did!
She knows what she's talking about, and she's very helpful.

analysis.name: Lawrence Solum
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. But trending upward recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 3 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Professor Solum is an excellent example of how you need a fun, smart professor teaching a dull, difficult course.  He obviously loves teaching, he is frighteningly familiar with the Federal Rules and Statutes, and is very good at clarifying complex matters.  I would take him again the first chance I get.

Best professor ever! Takes a little getting used to because he comes off a little strange at first. It becomes an endearing quality. He actually wants his students to achieve and is not dismissive. It has been a real pleasure having him as my professor
He is an awful teacher. Doesn't allow students to ask questions in class. Has bouts of brilliance but most of the time students have no idea what is going on. Avoid him at all costs!

analysis.name: Shannon Moritz
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 9 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Good classes, understood what I needed to understand

Unclear, arbitrary, and capricious grading style. It is extremely difficult to figure out what she wants because her feedback is all questions instead of direct statements. She also has a strict word count, making it difficult (if not impossible) to incorporate all of the things she complains about in her feedback. These are 100% unaltered facts.

She is awesome. As the director of legal writing Moritz knows her stuff. She can be distant and formal. However, she really is a sweet and kind woman if you get to know her. Her office hours are not very helpful. She will see mistakes during office hours & not tell you. Your best bet is to utilize her TAs, and to get proof reading & get advice.

Professor Moritz is very intelligent and explains things very well.  I feel that my writing has greatly improved since being in her class.  She wants her students to become better legal writers, and she truly cares about her students.

Professor Moritz is the best! She is always available to meet with you and really does care about how well you do in the course and teaching you. She also teaches in the clearest manner. Everything you need to know, she gives you.

analysis.name: Travis McDade
analysis.verdict: Solid pick — well-liked with reasonable difficulty. But trending upward recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Professor McDade keeps you engaged (and loves football)

Professor McDade is an inspiring professor who helped me love the art of writing. He brings an air of serious academic work to what is usually a mechanical professional education. His lay writing class would better be titled Legal Journalism but is well worth the credits.

I didn't take his class back in 2012, but if what that review says is true, it's definitely changed. Professor McDade has a calm and collected attitude towards everything and is very helpful if you don't understand a concept at first. The readings for Cultural Property Law was very interesting and he makes sure to present good slideshows for them.

Doesn't seem terribly interested in lecturing, but very helpful if you have a specific question or topic you want to work on. Keeps things as lighthearted as possible, given the incredibly boring material that has to be covered.

analysis.name: Peter Maggs
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 22 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: I should have come to his office hours more often.  I was having some personal problems in my life and felt like I let him down by not trying hard enough in his class.  He was the main reason I  decided to come to the College of Law.

Maggs is the best!  He is very helpful if you seek him out.  He teaches socratic all the time.  He is brilliant and his classes are organized beyond belief.  It is worth it just for Maggs' last stand (when he recites every case of the semester from Contracts in the last class (50 minutes) by memory).

A scary, scary man.  Why?  Because he is so much more intelligent than anyone else you will ever meet that he is almost super-human.  He is knows contract law inside and out and is wonderfully generous in sharing that knowledge with his students.  His class was tough, but a privlege.

Socially stunted, but absolutely amazing as a teacher- if you make the effort to contact him.  You have to understand how he thinks though. If you understand how he thinks, the class becomes crystal clear.  It is the getting there that is the hard part.  I half-understood him by the end of contracts, but after trademark, I was guessing his exam Q's

Professor Maggs is an unmatched scholar of law, but this is a rating of Maggs as a teacher.  As a teacher, he's still committed to using a textbook that is incomprehensible and disorganized in its current edition.  Like the textbook author, Maggs disproportionately teaches from the restatement.  Although he's a bit offputting, use his office hours.

analysis.name: Michael Moore
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews — check if this fits your learning style. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 12 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: I can understand why people find his class to be confusing, but I refuse to call his exam arbitray - torts is a broad subject, and his exam covered the vast majority of what we learned, and I feel my results reflected my understanding of the material...I smell C's, shot egos, and denial

So his tests are arbitrary, so you'll find yourself actually unable to understand his thoughts at times...probably the best and most reputable scholar the law school has, and you should feel grateful he comes down from on high to teach you anything.  if you're into ethics, he's your man. if you have a question, he will answer it...don't be scurred.

i left this class feeling ill on many occasions just because I didn't understand the lectures.  I did all of the reading and did the work, but Moore was on a level way above me.  He seemed to teach torts from the more obscure aspects and never really covered the basic information.

Very intelligent professor but he is so smart he's all over the place, and will intellectually dominate you with his long-winded sentences filled with complicated words.  My advice: bring a stenographer to class with you.
Genius at law and wants you to know it.  More interested in this than actually teaching you anything.  Leaves everyone behind.  Traditionally has the lowest grades vs. his peers.

analysis.name: Nicola Sharpe
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Conflated almost every issue. Teaching style can be considered disorganized at best. Has a difficult time distinguishing issues and gets caught up in trivial details and side issues that are not relevant to the class (she spends more time on material unimportant to the class). Showing up and paying attention has a detrimental effect.

I took Prof. Nicola Sharpe's Counsel Practicum, which is two weeks after finals. Class was tough, both because of its timing and Professor Sharpe. Since it's accelerated, class is everyday and the work is heavy. Prof Sharpe is excellent. She is a trove of knowledge and an incredibly educated and wicked smart person. Highly recommend
Terrific professor, very engaged with the materials, presents the information in a clear and easy to understand manner, always available to discuss cases

analysis.name: John Nowak
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 13 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: I love the guy, but class was confusing and dull (though he knows this and is very realistic about it).  The hornbook is essentially a more orgainized and easy to follow version of his class and is a must if you are having trouble following the lecture.  Grab the concise hornbook and you can teach yourself the entire semester in an afternoon.

Excellent professor.  Rambles a bit, but is entertaining when he does.  Buy his hornbook, use it even after Con Law I, because it is well written and concise.  He takes a complicated topic and makes it simple and easy to understand.  His class lectures are key to his exam though.  Make sure you get the hypotheticals down.

Nothing wrong with Nowak, he's a funny guy, but he tends to ramble and is hard to follow.  He randomly interjects comments about pointless things into his lectures and goes off on tangents so that it tends to be very hard to take notes in his class.  I would recommend avoiding his classes if possible.

Studying for con law was the most stressful experience of my 1L year.  Nowak is a nice guy and entertaining in lecture, but often strays off topic and can be difficult to follow.  Overall though, he is charming and seems to have a kind heart.

Great guy--funny as hell--but often hard to follow. He skips around a bit but in the end it seems to come together. Definitely look at the horn book or a study aid. His tests aren't bad but the instructions are longer than the questions.

analysis.name: Yanfeng Ouyang
analysis.verdict: Good teacher but expect a challenge. But trending upward recently
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 7 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Enthusiastic about the subject. Approachable and will gladly take the time to go over concepts with students, either when asked in class or individually during office hours.  Unfortunately the tests are long and difficult, with very little homework practice/feedback to prepare you.

Agree, he's a good story teller, but not a good instructor for teaching. He does tried a lot to explain his lecture, but still his explanation is confuse. The point is easy, but he just explained in a complex way. I spend over 6 hours on each of his hw. He uses a wordy way in hw problem and makes it burry and confuse. I don't recommend his class.

Great lectures, and interesting topics covered that you won't get anywhere else. The homeworks can be intense, and there isn't much help. You may easily fail a homework, which can be trouble when there are only five. You must go to office hours for the projects, or you will do poorly.

He's a good storyteller, but not clear on describing hw. The points that he taught on class does not have many examples to support. I'm struggling with hw every time after class. It's more useful to read the textbook instead of attending his class.

Professor gave amazing lectures. If you are interested in Transportation Engineering, please do not miss this course! The homework and midterm are intense but helpful. And the group projects are very interesting.

analysis.name: Paul Stancil
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 7 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Good man: clearly wants you to understand the topic but the goal isn't always accomplished. Go to his office hours, listen to others' questions, grab the E&E from the beginning. He wants the minutiae from the casebook, which is a bit odd given the subject but he's testing your reading/comprehension as well. His final feels "gotcha" - very hard.

All around great guy. He is a difficult teacher, and if you don't like to read a lot, then you shouldn't take the class. That said, if you want to take a class from a really smart, really friendly, really cool guy and don't mind doing some work, he's your man.

He is definitely more enjoyable outside of class. Pretty condescending in class. Always looking to show how smart he is. His final is ridiculous- wanted deep discussion of topics we skimmed over in class. Nice guy out of class, just not a good teacher.

Agree with the comments below, though it's clear he's taught more classes recently.  Great guy, odd sensibilities and sense of humor, and generally very passionate.  I think he could be a great teacher someday.

The grade scale provided is unfair for Professor Stancil. He may be unclear, unhelpful at office hours, and an extremely hard professor. however he is AMAZING and will CHANGE YOUR LIFE. Best professor ever.

analysis.name: Richard Ross
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Based on 14 reviews — decent sample but could shift

review_highlights.comment: Property is very much a love it or hate it subject, but Ross does a very good job at sparking interest. His class is more policy focused than a lot of other 1L courses, which i found refreshing, and his training as a historian can definintly make you feel like you're in an undergrad history couse at times.
He's distinguished, chill, and surprisingly knows a lot about pop culture. Property law is, in itself, a dull subject, but he tries his best to make it interesting.

Early Legal Cultures of America is more like an undergrad history seminar than a law school class. The class focuses on the development of early American legal culture. Your grade is based on two 15-page papers (NO bluebook format/extra research outside the reading you need to do for class), and class participation. I found this class to be relaxed

To Ross you are just another number. He does not care about his students and could care less if they pass. He has read from the same outline for years, does not change his hypotheticals, and is here to cash a paycheck. Property could have been a cool subject to learn about but he prevents that in every way. If you fall behind good luck. The worst.

He should not be teaching. While he is very bright, he made it very clear he did not want to answer any questions I had and was incredibly rude and disrespectful to students. While we all can't be as smart as he is, he is here to teach. That is his job.

analysis.name: Robin Wilson
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 4 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Prof. Wilson has A LOT going on, at all times. This can make her seem a bit unorganized at times. She is a fair grader and really takes the time to help out students and point them in the right direction. She is pretty transparent with class expectations. She's super nice, just give her a chance and go talk to her sometime if you're struggling!
Great professor who cares about her students and wants them to succeed. Highly recommend her!
Doesn't seem to care about her students or her clients. Do not reccomend.

analysis.name: Suja Thomas
analysis.verdict: Mixed reviews — experiences vary a lot between students
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 7 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: Great teacher (I found myself remembering everything she taught us months later), but a little dismissive. She does, however, root for your success. She also doesn't test the Erie doctrine- make of that what you will.

Decent in class, but nothing special. Material translated well to the final, which is a plus. Weird syllabus organization - didn't cover Erie, which is strange. Really poor in office hours - like... really, really poor. Exam review was the least helpful of all the exam reviews I've ever done. You just read your exam yourself and get no feedback.

Professor Suja A. Thomas is an incredible professor and a valued asset to the College of Law. Her Employment Discrimination course was my favorite class in law school; I learned so much and she made the materials extremely interesting.

Sports law was a cool class. Every week, there is a speaker who talks to the class about how they became sports lawyers. We had readings each week covering different issues in sports law. Grade determined by two papers, one being an interview with a sports law figure and the other being a research paper. Wish the second paper was due before finals.
Professor Thomas is extremely smart but she does talk fast. It can be hard to keep up also what she means. That said, she is good intentioned and wants you to succeed.

analysis.name: Simrun Sethi
analysis.verdict: Below average reviews — check if this fits your learning style. And trending downward
analysis.confidence_detail: Only 6 reviews — take these numbers with a grain of salt

review_highlights.comment: She's patient and I feel indebted, I went through depression this semester and she was understanding when I told her because I didn't do too well. She gives great advice and is very accessible outside of class. I had her last semester too and I think our entire class can agree that she's good. Sometimes she's unclear but she'll explain if u ask

Super easy class because it is super "obvious" material. Grading rubrics are very hard to understand and she changes her criteria on the fly, at least she is easy to reach out to and get a hold of. It is clear that she has her favorite students.

Gives a lot of attitude when people don't understand the material/ deliverables. She gets very defensive when we try to give constructive criticisms about her teaching style. The classes are not very organized and she doesn't engage the class too much. The assignments are too confusing to understand because she doesn't explain what needs/wants.
